agent2 (Round 2 summarized): The most likely diagnosis is tuberous sclerosis complex due to the coexistence of bilateral renal angiomyolipomas, recurrent hematuria, flank pain, and seizures — all hallmark features consistent with TSC. This triad of neurologic involvement (epilepsy), renal lesions (bilateral angiomyolipomas causing bleeding/pain), and systemic nature makes TSC uniquely fitting compared to differential diagnoses like von Hippel-Lindau or autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease.  

Final Answer:

\boxed{C}

The most probable diagnosis is tuberous sclerosis complex because the patient exhibits bilateral renal angiomyolipomas, recurrent gross hematuria, flank pain, and seizures—which collectively represent classic signs of TSC. These symptoms strongly correlate with established clinical criteria for TSC, distinguishing it from conditions such as Von Hippel-Lindau or polycystic kidney disease.
